original_abstract: The ability to associate images with natural language sentences that describe what is depicted in them is a hallmark of image understanding, and a prerequisite for applications such as sentence-based image search. In analogy to image search, we propose to frame sentence-based image annotation as the task of ranking a given pool of captions. We introduce a new benchmark collection for sentence-based image description and search, consisting of 8,000 images that are each paired with five different captions which provide clear descriptions of the salient entities and events. We introduce a number of systems that perform quite well on this task, even though they are only based on features that can be obtained with minimal supervision. Our results clearly indicate the importance of training on multiple captions per image, and of capturing syntactic (word order-based) and semantic features of these captions. We also perform an in-depth comparison of human and automatic evaluation metrics for this task, and propose strategies for collecting human judgments cheaply and on a very large scale, allowing us to augment our collection with additional relevance judgments of which captions describe which image. Our analysis shows that metrics that consider the ranked list of results for each query image or sentence are significantly more robust than metrics that are based on a single response per query. Moreover, our study suggests that the evaluation of ranking-based image description systems may be fully automated.
humanized_abstract: Sentence-based image annotation is an important aspect of image understanding. It is necessary for many vision applications such as sentence-based image search. Image search has its corresponding task of ranking candidate sentences for sentence-based image annotation. In this work, we present a new benchmark, a dataset called Flickr30k, which contains 8,000 images paired with 5 captions describing various objects and activities shown in the images. In addition, we propose and compare several systems that work very well in ranking captions to describe an image. All the systems that we compare use very basic features that can be obtained with minimal supervision. Therefore, the good performance that they achieve on the Flickr30k dataset verifies the importance of having multiple captions for training an image description system and of considering the syntax and semantics of the captions. We also perform an experimental evaluation of different metrics that can be used to measure the quality of ranking descriptions of an image or the relevance of captions w.r.t. a sentence. We give recommendations on how to collect sufficient human judgements in an economical fashion and in large quantities, and on how to expand our dataset. Our results indicate that ranking metrics are significantly better than the single response evaluation metrics. Therefore, our proposed evaluation metric is fully automatic.
